metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge and fix both C2 blockers in the child plan body: (a) align the key used in reconcile.py's hard_delete payload with the key apply_inbound_delete reads, and (b) wire the create_after_hard_delete follow-on into reconcile_once's applier loop (the TODO at apply_inbound.py:560-561 must be resolved, not just referenced).
findings.finding: C2's success criterion ('hard-delete re-create works') is stated as achievable, but the live code contains two unacknowledged blockers that together make the mechanism unreachable: (1) reconcile.py:434 emits payload key 'reason' but apply_inbound.py:535 reads payload.get('probe_outcome', 'out_of_window'), so the hard_delete branch is silently skipped every pass; (2) apply_inbound.py:560-561 contains an explicit TODO noting the create_after_hard_delete follow-on is not wired into reconcile_once. The plan's C2 criterion ('hard-delete re-create works') asserts end-to-end operation without addressing either gap.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Band C — 'hard-delete -> re-create works'; Acceptance Criteria — 'C2 (hard-delete re-create)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the C2 child plan, add to the 'What' section: (1) standardize on one key (e.g. 'probe_outcome') in both reconcile.py:434 and the applier's payload, removing the mismatch; (2) in reconcile_once's follow-on dispatch loop, add an arm that converts a create_after_hard_delete follow-on into a typed Mutation(outbound, create) and dispatches it before the pass closes.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the D1 blast-radius assertion signal with the actual census dict shape: either update D1 to assert `breaker.allowed == False` (matching classify.census()) or add a `refused` key to the census payload and reference it consistently.
findings.finding: D1's blast-radius breaker scenario states it should be 'asserted via the `refused` census/verdict the breaker emits'. The codebase's `classify.census()` emits a `breaker` sub-dict with key `allowed` (bool), not a `refused` field. The `refused` flag is on `WalkResult` (binding_walk.py:77), not in the census dict. An executor asserting for a `refused` key in the emitted census record will find nothing and either fail incorrectly or must invent an assertion path not described in the plan.
findings.location: D1 (close-ferreous-cardinal) — Signal definitions, Blast-radius breaker bullet; classify.py census() function; binding_walk.py WalkResult
findings.suggested_fix: Change D1's breaker signal bullet to: 'assert `census["breaker"]["allowed"] == False` (breaker tripped) and `census["breaker"]["acting_fraction"] > 0.10`.' Alternatively, emit `refused: not allowed` in `classify.census()` and update D1 to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a merge-order constraint between C1 (a713) and B1 (a118) for outbound_fields.py _local_matches_prev: specify that C1's conflict-detection code must be authored against the post-B1 get_baseline API, and either land B1 first or develop C1 on a branch that includes B1's changes.
findings.finding: outbound_fields.py (_local_matches_prev function) is concurrently modified by both B1 (a118, re-pointing the function to read get_baseline) and C1 (a713, adding both-sides conflict detection to the same function). The parent cross-child coordination section mentions only apply_outbound.py for C1/C3 overlap; it does not mention this shared-function overlap between B1 and C1.
findings.location: child a118-fa8e-547a-49ba (CODE: 're-point outbound_fields._local_matches_prev ... from prev_snapshot to BindingStore.get_baseline') and child a713-b4e0-4662-4e6f (What: 'outbound_fields.py (_local_matches_prev / _diff_fields)')
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly in both a713 and a118 that C1 must be developed on top of (or rebased onto) B1's get_baseline swap, so C1's three-way-diff logic uses the correct baseline source from the start.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in D1 (d01e) that the C1 matrix scenario must be validated AFTER B1 is flipped (not just after C1 lands), so the conflict signal is tested against the accurate per-binding baseline, not prev_snapshot.
findings.finding: C1 (a713) can be deployed before B1 (a118) flips _local_matches_prev to use get_baseline, causing C1's both-sides conflict detection to run against prev_snapshot (the old, less-accurate arbiter) rather than the per-binding baseline. In this intermediate state, C1 produces conflict signals of lower accuracy than intended, and the D1 harness (which references C1's signal) may validate against degraded behavior without knowing it.
findings.location: child a713-b4e0-4662-4e6f (What: '_local_matches_prev / _diff_fields'); child a118-fa8e-547a-49ba (CODE: 're-point _local_matches_prev to get_baseline'); parent cross-child note ('C1 relates_to B1')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to d01e's ordering note: 'C1 matrix scenario requires B1 (baseline_consumer_swap flag flipped) to be live before the D1 matrix run, to validate conflict detection against the per-binding baseline rather than prev_snapshot.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to the C4 design, verify (with a probe run or ACLI source/docs) whether the ACLI Go subprocess emits the Retry-After header value in a parseable stdout/stderr form when Jira returns 429, and if not, specify the alternative path (e.g., REST-layer fallback via `_call_with_backoff`, or adding Retry-After support to the `_call_with_retry` ACLI client path) so the 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After' criterion is achievable.
findings.finding: The Band C success criterion 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After' (SC-C / C4) is an unverified capability assertion against the ACLI subprocess path. The live `_call_with_retry` in `dispatch_one.py` (lines 100-144) uses fixed delays [1, 2, 4] s and does NOT inspect Retry-After. Retry-After is only handled in `_call_with_backoff` (`acli_subprocess.py:236-286`) which covers the `urllib.error.HTTPError` REST surface — a different code path. ACLI runs as a Go subprocess; Python receives only a return code plus stdout/stderr text, not HTTP headers. Whether the Go ACLI binary propagates a Retry-After value in a parseable form has not been demonstrated in the plan.
findings.location: Success Criteria → C. Latent gaps closed: 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After'; Acceptance Criteria → C4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the D1 setup-probe rationale: `client.delete_issue` raises `PermissionError` on 403 (it does NOT return a soft `{"status":"deleted"}`); the probe should call `client.delete_issue` and assert it does NOT raise `PermissionError`, not that it returns a specific dict value.
findings.finding: The D1 harness spec (close-ferreous-cardinal) asserts that 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError', but the actual code at acli.py:721-723 raises PermissionError — it does not swallow it. The integration surface behavior is mischaracterized, which could mislead the harness implementor into writing the setup probe around the wrong failure mode.
findings.location: D1 ticket (close-ferreous-cardinal) — Setup probe section: 'assert client.delete_issue returns {"status":"deleted"} (NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace: '(NOT a 403 soft-fail — `acli.py:716-720` swallows PermissionError)' with '(a 403 surfaces as `PermissionError` raised from `acli.py:721-723` — the probe should catch or assert no `PermissionError` is raised, confirming delete permission is present)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `A3 precedes B1` (or `A3 blocks B1 streak-start`) edge to the AC dependency-edges bullet so the sequencing constraint is enforceable from the AC alone.
findings.finding: The Cross-child coordination section explicitly states 'A3's live remediation pauses the reconciler, which resets B1's >=10-consecutive-clean-shadow-pass streak — sequence them,' but the AC dependency-edges list ('A1->A2, A1->A3, A2->A3; C1 relates_to B1; D1 relates_to B1/C1/C2/C3/C4') omits any A3↔B1 sequencing edge. An implementer reading only the AC would not know this constraint exists and could run A3 during B1's streak window.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Dependency edges wired' bullet vs. 'Cross-child coordination — SEQUENCING' section
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the dependency-edges bullet to: 'A1->A2, A1->A3, A2->A3; A3 precedes B1 (A3 must complete before B1's clean-shadow-pass streak begins); C1 relates_to B1; D1 relates_to B1/C1/C2/C3/C4.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify SC-C's Retry-After language to match C4's conditional: e.g. 'bulk-path 429 backs off with jittered exponential backoff; honors Retry-After header if the C4 probe confirms ACLI surfaces it on the subprocess path'.
findings.finding: The epic-level Success Criterion C states 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After' as a flat, unconditional assertion, but the C4 child ticket (lambswool-shaded-eider) correctly identifies that Retry-After availability on the ACLI subprocess path is UNVERIFIED and conditions the implementation on a required probe. The SC overstates certainty about an unverified external capability.
findings.location: Success Criteria section, item C: 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After'; AC line: 'C4 (bulk-path 429 parity)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change SC-C from 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After' to 'bulk-path 429 backs off correctly (Retry-After honored if available per C4 probe; jittered exponential backoff otherwise)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the conflict-record event schema (event_type, required payload fields, and whether it reuses BRIDGE_ALERT) in C1's AC and cross-reference the exact key/field name in D1's C1 signal definition — do not defer to 'reference C1 at implementation time'.
findings.finding: D1 (close-ferreous-cardinal) instructs the agent to 'assert against the exact key/field C1 lands — reference C1, do not hard-code an unverified reason="conflict"'. C1 (feline-wandering-dassierat) never defines the conflict-record's event schema (event_type key, payload fields, whether it is a BRIDGE_ALERT or a separate event). The agent is told to look up the schema at C1 implementation time, but C1's ACs only say 'emits a conflict record (comment/alert)' with no key/field vocabulary. This is both an undefined-referenced-schema gap and an instruction-locality antipattern.
findings.location: D1 (close-ferreous-cardinal) — Signal definitions section, C1 bullet; C1 (feline-wandering-dassierat) — Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In C1 (feline-wandering-dassierat), add an AC line: 'The conflict record is a BRIDGE_ALERT event with data={"kind": "field_conflict", "fields": [...], "local_value": ..., "jira_value": ...}' (or equivalent concrete schema). Mirror that exact schema reference in D1's C1 signal bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the parent AC wording with C4's conditional outcome OR add a remainder task: if probe shows Retry-After unavailable, file a follow-up to negotiate Retry-After availability or escalate the mechanism gap so the parent metric is not declared met on a conditional.
findings.finding: C4 (943f) introduces a conditional path where Retry-After is NOT honored if the probe shows it unavailable, but the parent AC says 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After' — an unconditional statement. The child's fallback (jittered backoff without Retry-After) does not satisfy the parent criterion's observable outcome as written.
findings.location: child 943f-8303-829b-443a, AC line: '429 backs off with jittered exponential backoff; honors Retry-After IFF the probe confirmed availability'
findings.suggested_fix: Restate the parent AC for C4 as 'bulk-path 429 applies server-dictated wait (Retry-After if surfaced by ACLI, otherwise jittered backoff); probe result recorded' — or require C4 to file a follow-up ticket if Retry-After is unavailable, so the gap is tracked rather than silently closed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the metric mismatch: either (a) update the parent AC to 'zero UNRESOLVED faults (triaged items documented and suppressed in fsck output)' and add a fsck --suppress-triaged flag, or (b) require A3 to actually repair or suppress all fault classes so fsck exits with zero output.
findings.finding: A3 (34b1) redefines the parent's 'zero faults' metric: 'triaged+documented orphans count as resolved' — meaning fsck may still emit ORPHAN_EVENT or MISSING_CREATE entries for triaged items. The parent AC says 'live-store rebar fsck reports zero SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE', implying the tool exits zero, not just that unresolved items are annotated.
findings.location: child 34b1-e19c-35be-4392, AC line: 'Live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults (triaged+documented orphans count as resolved)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fsck suppression mechanism (e.g., a triage annotation file) so that triaged MISSING_CREATE/ORPHAN_EVENT items are excluded from fsck's output count, allowing the tool to report zero while the triage record is preserved separately. Update the parent AC to reference this mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a coordination note to the parent cross-child section (or to A1/A2 directly) specifying that reducer/_cache.py prepare_event_files must be merged in order (A1 first, A2 on top) and that a joint review of the merged function is required before A3 can land.
findings.finding: reducer/_cache.py (prepare_event_files, listing paths ~line 97 and ~line 102) is concurrently modified by two children — A1 (b306, adding is_active_event filter to both listing paths) and A2 (36d1, adding include_retired param to prepare_event_files signature and both listing paths) — without any cross-child coordination note in the parent or in either child.
findings.location: child b306-d68c-8ded-4bff (Scope: 'reducer/_cache.py — exclude *.retired in BOTH listing paths') and child 36d1-0f63-cae2-4b4c (Scope: 'reducer/_cache.py (prepare_event_files both listing paths)')
findings.suggested_fix: Land A1 (b306) first (it is already A2's depends_on); require A2 (36d1) to rebase on A1 and verify both changes coexist in prepare_event_files before merging A2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a coordination note for fsck.py to the parent cross-child section: A2 lands first (it is A3's depends_on), A3 builds repair on top of A2's orphan-detect; the orphan-detect API/hook point is agreed between A2 and A3 before development.
findings.finding: _commands/fsck.py is concurrently modified by A2 (36d1, adding orphan-detect + rebuild trigger) and A3 (34b1, adding --repair/--dry-run/--limit CLI flags + per-ticket marker logic + _check_snapshot is_active_event guard). Neither the parent cross-child coordination section nor either child specifies merge order or joint review for this file.
findings.location: child 36d1-0f63-cae2-4b4c (Scope: '_commands/fsck.py (orphan-detect + rebuild trigger)') and child 34b1-e19c-35be-4392 (Scope: '_commands/fsck.py (--repair/--dry-run/--limit + per-ticket marker + per-snapshot rewrite)')
findings.suggested_fix: In A2 (36d1), explicitly define the orphan-detect function/hook signature in fsck.py that A3 will consume for repair dispatch, so A3 does not re-implement detection from scratch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal dependency edge (e.g. 'A3 must precede B1 streak start, or B1's streak window must be documented as paused/reset when A3 runs') to the Acceptance Criteria dependency-edges bullet, and/or encode the constraint in both child ACs (A3's AC explicitly states it must not run during an active B1 streak; B1's AC explicitly states it resets the streak counter when the reconciler is paused).
findings.finding: The cross-child coordination section correctly identifies that A3's live remediation resets B1's consecutive-clean-shadow-pass streak and prescribes sequencing them, but this constraint is advisory prose in the parent plan only — it is not encoded in either child's acceptance criteria as a hard precondition or dependency edge. The AC section names A1→A2, A1→A3, A2→A3 dependency edges but does not add an A3-must-not-overlap-B1-streak-window edge. If A3 executes while B1's streak is in progress, the streak resets silently and the Phase-3 consumer swap gate is delayed without any AC failing.
findings.location: Cross-child coordination section — 'SEQUENCING: A3's live remediation pauses the reconciler...'; Acceptance Criteria — 'Dependency edges wired' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal operational gate to A3 (34b1): before starting the live remediation pass, check whether B1's streak window is active (query last N baseline_shadow_check events); abort if streak >= 5 (halfway) to avoid resetting it.
findings.finding: There is no formal dependency edge (depends_on or blocks) between A3 (34b1) and B1 (a118) to enforce the 'do NOT run A3's live pass during B1's streak window' sequencing rule. The rule exists only as prose in the parent body and a note in a118 — a naive scheduler, CI automation, or operator can violate it silently.
findings.location: parent cross-child coordination section ('SEQUENCING: A3's live remediation pauses the reconciler, which resets B1's >=10-consecutive-clean-shadow-pass streak'); child a118-fa8e-547a-49ba COORDINATION note
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-flight check to A3's remediation runbook/script: 'query B1 streak length; if streak >= 1, defer A3 live pass until B1's flip is complete OR the streak is at zero.' Mirror this as a note in 34b1's safety section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In B1, either derive the 10-pass threshold from observed shadow-pass data (cite the measurement) or mark it explicitly as a configurable parameter with a rationale comment, and note the derivation basis (e.g., 'covers ~3 hours of reconciler cycles at 20-min cadence').
findings.finding: The '>=10 consecutive clean shadow passes' threshold governing the B1 Phase-3 consumer swap readiness gate is an asserted heuristic with no derivation from measurement or data. It is a tunable correctness gate that could easily need adjustment based on observed pass frequency, yet the plan neither notes it as configurable nor plans a measurement step to validate it.
findings.location: Context section: 'the per-binding baseline runs dual-write SHADOW — the Phase-3 consumer swap is not yet live'; Band B AC: 'B1 (Phase-3 baseline consumer swap; agent performs its live validation with cleanup)'; Cross-child coordination: 'resets B1's >=10-consecutive-clean-shadow-pass streak'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to B1's child ticket: 'The >=10-pass gate was chosen to cover N reconciler cycles spanning at least Y hours of normal load; record this derivation in a config comment. The threshold MUST be a named constant (e.g. SHADOW_PASS_GATE = 10), not an inline literal, so it can be adjusted without a search-and-replace.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion to B1's rollback procedure: explicitly state that accumulated `baseline`/`baseline_advanced_at` fields in `bindings.json` are inert after flag revert (since `get_baseline` returns None for a non-dict/absent entry and the `prev_snapshot` path never calls it), and confirm no manual cleanup of bindings.json is required — so operators have a verifiable known-good state after rollback.
findings.finding: B1's (lofty-beech-twang) rollback procedure reverts `reconciler.baseline_consumer_swap` to false but never asserts the schema STATE of `bindings.json` after rollback. During the dual-write shadow period, every binding entry accumulates `baseline` and `baseline_advanced_at` fields. These fields persist indefinitely after the flag is reverted; the plan does not state whether this is a known-safe intermediate state or requires cleanup.
findings.location: lofty-beech-twang (B1) OPERATIONAL section: 'Rollback rehearsed: (a) revert the flag to false and confirm the reconciler reverts to prev_snapshot arbitration with no divergence, AND (b) force one --mode dry-run pass confirming zero mutations; record both workflow run-ids.' / AC: '[OPERATIONAL] Flip only after both gates; rollback rehearsed (flag-revert + dry-run run-ids recorded).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to B1's rollback section: 'Post-rollback schema state: bindings.json retains accumulated baseline/baseline_advanced_at fields from the shadow period; these are inert (get_baseline returns None → local-wins branch, identical to the pre-swap behavior). No manual cleanup of bindings.json is required. Verify by running one --mode dry-run pass and confirming mutation_count==0 and no baseline_shadow_divergence alerts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership of the Risk #5 single-hop-status-transition acceptance decision to one child (or D1/B1 ADR) so the parent AC checkbox has a concrete artifact to cite.
findings.finding: No child formally records the Risk #5 acceptance decision (single-hop status transitions explicitly OOS). The parent AC lists this as a checkbox, but none of the 9 children owns creating an ADR entry, a design-doc note, or any other durable artifact that would allow the checkbox to be ticked at completion.
findings.location: parent AC — 'Risk #5 (single-hop status transitions) explicitly accepted / out of scope'; no child in roster
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to d01e's (D1) or a118's (B1) ADR/design-note deliverable: 'Single-hop status transitions are accepted out of scope (Risk #5); see this ADR.' This gives the completion verifier a tickable artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the chosen RC2b fix approach in A2's AC (e.g., 'reject writes whose HLC is below the current snapshot horizon at commit time and force a re-fetch' or similar) so the executor implements the decided strategy rather than selecting one ad hoc.
findings.finding: A2's acceptance criterion 'carries an approach decision' defers the design choice for the RC2b horizon-safe fix to the executor without stating the chosen approach, making it a placeholder rather than a decision.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — '[ ] Band A children landed: A1 (`*.retired`), A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision), A3'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)' with 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; approach: <chosen mechanism, e.g. reject-and-rebase on horizon check at commit, or fence token on tickets-branch HEAD>)' so the approach is a recorded decision, not a deferred one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'comprehensive live validation' in D1's AC with an explicit enumeration of the required test flows (e.g., create, update, same-field conflict, hard-delete→re-create, allowlist-drop observability, bulk-path 429 recovery, bilateral cleanup) so the criterion is evaluable against a fixed checklist.
findings.finding: D1's acceptance criterion uses the subjective term 'comprehensive' and the undefined scope 'full bidirectional surface' without enumerating the test scenarios, leaving two agents free to produce wildly different coverage and still claim the criterion is met.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'D. Proven live' and Acceptance Criteria — '[ ] Band D: D1 (comprehensive live validation + guaranteed bilateral cleanup)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC A3's wording to include the tool name: e.g., 'A3 (live-store remediation: `rebar fsck` reports zero SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE)'.
findings.finding: Success Criteria A names the validation tool as 'rebar fsck' ('live-store `rebar fsck` reports zero…'), but AC A3 drops the tool name entirely ('A3 (live-store remediation to zero fsck)'). An implementer reading only the AC cannot determine which tool/command achieves the zero-fsck criterion.
findings.location: Success Criteria section (Band A) vs. Acceptance Criteria — A3 line
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC A3 to: 'A3 (live-store remediation: `rebar fsck` reports zero SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Normalize the RC2b concept label — pick one term (e.g., 'sub-horizon append' or 'horizon-safe append') and use it consistently in Context, Success Criteria A, and AC A2.
findings.finding: The concept described in Context as 'concurrent write on a stale base gets an HLC below a peer's snapshot horizon; replay's positional skip drops it' is named 'sub-horizon append' in Success Criteria A but simply 'RC2b horizon-safe' in AC A2. Three different phrasings for the same concept across three sections creates terminology drift that may confuse cross-referencing.
findings.location: Context (RC2b description) vs. Success Criteria A ('sub-horizon append') vs. AC A2 ('RC2b horizon-safe')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-remediation step to A3: take a snapshot/backup of the tickets branch (e.g., `git tag pre-a3-remediation <HEAD>` or copy the branch) before any destructive writes, and document the rollback procedure (restore from tag/copy) in the A3 child plan.
findings.finding: A3 live-store remediation is an explicitly destructive/irreversible operation on ~2,400 corrupted live records, but the plan describes no pre-remediation backup/snapshot or back-out procedure. If the remediation script has a bug or is interrupted mid-run, the store is left in an unknown state with no recovery path.
findings.location: Success Criteria section, AC line: 'A3 (live-store remediation to zero fsck)'; Cross-child coordination: 'A3's live remediation pauses the reconciler'
findings.suggested_fix: In A3's child plan, add a mandatory pre-flight step: `git tag pre-a3-remediation $(git rev-parse tickets)` on the tickets branch before the remediation script runs, and document 'rollback: git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation' as the explicit back-out path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a new ADR (e.g., ADR-003x) as an explicit deliverable for A2 documenting the RC2b sub-horizon append approach decision, and reference it in A2's AC the same way band B references ADRs 0026-0029.
findings.finding: A2 is described as carrying 'an approach decision' (the RC2b horizon-safe concurrent-write solution), which is a new architectural contract for how sub-horizon appends are handled, but no ADR is cited or called out as a deliverable for A2.
findings.location: Context section: 'RC2b (band A)'; AC: 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief doc entry or ADR note for C4 recording the decision to honor Retry-After over the previous coarse 429 handling, and note any operator-configurable timeout bounds introduced.
findings.finding: C4 introduces a new behavioral contract (bulk-path 429 responses honor the Retry-After header), which is a change to observable rate-limit handling behavior and an implicit API-interaction contract, but no doc or ADR is mentioned for it.
findings.location: Context: 'coarse bulk 429 handling'; Success Criteria C: 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement in A2 to produce an ADR (or append to an existing ADR like 0026/0027) documenting the chosen RC2b horizon-safe concurrent-write approach, its invariants, and why alternatives were rejected.
findings.finding: The RC2b horizon-safe concurrent-write design (A2) is described as 'carries an approach decision,' meaning the architectural choice is unresolved, yet no ADR or design-doc update is called out for it. This is a novel concurrency-correctness decision (HLC sub-horizon append detection/prevention) that crosses the store and reconciler components.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Band A: 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)'; Acceptance Criteria AC line for A2
findings.suggested_fix: In the A2 child ticket, require: 'Document the selected approach in ADR-XXXX (or ADR-0026 appendix): what the HLC horizon invariant is, how sub-horizon appends are detected/rejected/deferred, and why the alternative approaches were ruled out.' Reference it from the Acceptance Criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the chosen approach for A2's RC2b fix in the parent plan (or confirm it is stated in the A2 child plan and reference it here) — the mechanism must identify where the guard runs (write time vs. replay), how it handles the race between the guard check and the merge, and what the failure mode is (reject-and-retry vs. epoch-bump vs. replay-reorder).
findings.finding: A2 (RC2b horizon-safe concurrent append) carries no stated approach — the plan says 'carries an approach decision' without identifying the chosen mechanism. This means the G6 mechanism-correctness and fitness-for-purpose criteria cannot be evaluated for A2: there is no logic/data-flow to reason through. The RC2b problem (concurrent write below snapshot horizon → positional skip → silent data loss) requires a concrete design choice among structurally different options (reject-stale-base at write time, epoch-bump guard on replay, HLC-horizon barrier, defer-to-next-pass), each with different ordering assumptions and failure modes.
findings.location: Context — 'RC2b (band A)'; Success Criteria — 'no concurrent sub-horizon append silently dropped (RC2b)'; Acceptance Criteria — 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which retry path(s) C4 targets — name dispatch_one._call_with_retry (REST path) and/or acli_subprocess._run_acli (subprocess path) explicitly, and for the subprocess path, state the mechanism by which Retry-After information will be extracted (ACLI stdout parsing, env-var injection, or accept-as-not-achievable) before committing to 'honors Retry-After' as the criterion.
findings.finding: C4's success criterion ('bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After') is underspecified relative to the codebase's two structurally distinct retry paths. dispatch_one._call_with_retry (the REST path through JiraAPIError) uses fixed delays with no Retry-After support. acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff reads Retry-After from urllib HTTPError.headers. _run_acli (the ACLI subprocess wrapper) retries CalledProcessError with fixed 2s/4s delays — HTTP response headers are not accessible at the subprocess level. The plan's C4 criterion does not name which path it targets, which leaves the approach unverifiable and risks the implementer patching the wrong path or claiming completion after fixing only the REST path while the ACLI subprocess path (the more common write path) remains coarse.
findings.location: Context — 'coarse bulk-path 429 handling'; Success Criteria — 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After'; Acceptance Criteria — 'C4 (bulk-path 429 parity)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-A3 backup step (e.g., `git tag pre-a3-remediation` on the tickets branch before any writes) and specify the post-rollback schema state assertion (e.g., 'after `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation`, fsck must report the original error set — no new corruption introduced') so A3's live remediation is fully reversible on partial failure.
findings.finding: A3 live-store remediation of ~2,400 corrupted records has no pre-operation backup/rollback anchor and no assertion of the store's schema state after a partial failure. If the repair run crashes or produces incorrect SNAPSHOTs mid-flight, there is no described path to recover to a known-good state. DATA LOSS RISK: a partial repair leaves an indeterminate mix of repaired and still-corrupted tickets with no recovery procedure.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Band A: 'live-store rebar fsck reports zero SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE'; AC line: 'A3 (live-store remediation to zero fsck)'
findings.suggested_fix: In A3's child plan, add a mandatory pre-flight step: `git tag pre-a3-remediation $(git rev-parse HEAD)` on the tickets branch before the remediation script runs. Document the rollback procedure ('on partial failure: git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation on the tickets branch; the store reverts to the pre-repair state') and the post-rollback assertion ('rebar fsck after rollback must show the same SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT count as before the run — no new errors added').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in A3's acceptance criteria that the live remediation runs in bounded batches (e.g., via `compact-all --limit=N` per run, with a resumability guarantee that re-running skips already-repaired tickets), rather than a single all-at-once transaction across all ~2,400 records.
findings.finding: The epic plan does not specify that A3's live-store remediation of ~2,400 records is batched or throttled. The compact-all infrastructure has a `--limit=N` flag, but the epic-level plan commits only to 'live-store rebar fsck reports zero' with no statement that A3 will use batched execution rather than a single all-at-once run.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Band A: 'live-store rebar fsck reports zero SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE'; AC line: 'A3 (live-store remediation to zero fsck)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either wire REB-530 as an explicit depends_on for a118 (B1) or document a fallback: if REB-530 is not complete at heal time, skip GC-after-grace deletion and file a follow-up, explicitly noting that the heal is partial without it.
findings.finding: B1 (a118) depends operationally on external ticket REB-530 (referenced as f8b5-2f30) for the 'GC-after-grace deletion' step of the capped live heal. This dependency is not listed as a depends_on edge, is not in the sibling roster, and has no fallback if REB-530 is incomplete — leaving B1's operational heal gate potentially blocked by an unwired external ticket.
findings.location: child a118-fa8e-547a-49ba, OPERATIONAL section: 'Capped live heal under bootstrap-strict incl. the GC-after-grace deletion (f8b5-2f30 -> REB-530)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'depends_on: f8b5-2f30 (REB-530)' to a118, or add a conditional in a118's operational section: 'If REB-530 is not merged, omit the GC-after-grace step and note the omission in the heal artifact log.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
